messages.content:
Create a retrieval-oriented summary for this section of a document.
Focus on what questions this section can answer: topics, entities, dates,
definitions, procedures, claims, tables, figures, metrics, and evidence types.
Do not invent facts. Return only the summary text.

Section metadata:
{
  "node_id": "0011",
  "title": "Partnerships as Marketing Channels",
  "page_index": 17,
  "page_range": "17"
}

Section text:
Partnerships as
Marketing Channels
Gaming studios are increasingly forging alliances
with technology companies, brands, and other
gaming companies, to strengthen their offerings,
reduce development costs, attract new players, and
enhance visibility.
A common type of partnership is co-development
where two developers or publishers collaborate with
each other or with an external vendor, to conceive,
develop, and launch a game title. The pooling of
resources and expertise results in reduced costs,
innovative gaming experiences, and access to other
markets. The sheer scale of modern big-budget
gaming and market saturation has necessitated
co-development, even among big studios. A
great example of successful co-development is
The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt which was produced by
CD Projekt Red and Warner Bros. Interactive. The
partnership was not only responsible for the game’s
stunning visuals but also its successful distribution in
North America.
Another type is a licensing agreement, typically
with a brand, where one company gives the studio
the rights to use its intellectual property (IP) or
characters, helping it to expand its reach to a non-
endemic audience and monetise its assets. This
partnering strategy is particularly useful to combat
the stark increase in user acquisition and game
development costs. Scopely’s Monopoly Go which
is based on Hasbro’s iconic board game is a fine
example of this strategy, generating US$2 billion in
revenue just 10 months after launch.
In 2023, some of the largest and most successful
film and TV franchises began as video games. These
include The Last of Us, Castlevania: Nocturne, The
Super Mario Bros. Movie, Twisted Metal, and Captain
Laserhawk: A Blood Dragon Remix. According to a
study by Deloitte, the share of theatrical box office
revenues from video game IP is expected to double
over the period 2023-2025, with most major video
streaming services including shows based on
games by 2025.
Yet another collaboration type is technology
partnerships where studios collaborate with large
technology companies such as Amazon, Google,
and Microsoft, or smaller gaming technology
(Gametech) companies, to integrate cutting-
edge solutions into their games. The acquisition of
Naughty Dog by Sony in 2001 is probably one the
first of such partnerships, giving the former access
to Sony’s powerful gaming consoles. The 2016
collaboration between game developer Valve and
technology company HTC, resulted in the creation
of the HTC Vive, a groundbreaking virtual reality (VR)
headset that was instrumental in bringing high-
quality VR gaming to the masses.
A common type of partnership
is co-development where
two developers or publishers
collaborate with each other
or with an external vendor, to
conceive, develop, and launch
a game title. The pooling of
resources and expertise results
in reduced costs, innovative
gaming experiences, and access
to other markets.
16
16
Book Online
Visit Site
Visit Site

message.content:
**Topics:** Gaming industry partnerships as marketing channels, co-development models, IP licensing, technology collaborations, cross-media adaptations (games to film/TV), cost reduction, market expansion, and user acquisition strategies.

**Entities:** 
- Studios/Publishers: CD Projekt Red, Warner Bros. Interactive, Scopely, Naughty Dog, Valve
- Technology Companies: Sony, HTC, Amazon, Google, Microsoft
- Brands: Hasbro
- Franchises/IPs/Products: The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt, Monopoly Go, The Last of Us, Castlevania: Nocturne, The Super Mario Bros. Movie, Twisted Metal, Captain Laserhawk: A Blood Dragon Remix, HTC Vive

**Dates:** 
- 2001: Sony's acquisition of Naughty Dog
- 2016: Valve and HTC collaboration
- 2023: Release of major game-based film/TV franchises
- 2023–2025: Deloitte projection window for box office revenue growth
- 10 months post-launch: Monopoly Go revenue milestone

**Definitions:** 
- Co-development: Collaboration between developers, publishers, or external vendors to jointly conceive, develop, and launch a game, pooling resources to reduce costs, drive innovation, and access new markets.
- Licensing agreement: A partnership where a brand grants a studio rights to use its intellectual property or characters to reach non-endemic audiences and monetize assets.
- Technology partnership: Collaboration with large tech firms or specialized Gametech companies to integrate cutting-edge hardware or software solutions into games.

**Procedures/Strategies:** Pooling development resources and expertise for joint production; leveraging licensed IP to offset rising user acquisition and development costs; integrating advanced tech via corporate partnerships; adapting game IP for film and television to diversify revenue streams and expand audience reach.

**Claims:** Partnerships strengthen game offerings, reduce development costs, attract new players, and enhance visibility. Co-development is now necessary due to the scale of modern big-budget gaming and market saturation. Licensing effectively combats rising UA and production expenses. Video game IP will significantly grow its share of theatrical box office revenue in the near term.

**Metrics:** Monopoly Go generated US$2 billion in revenue within 10 months of launch. Deloitte projects the share of theatrical box office revenues from video game IP will double between 2023 and 2025.

**Evidence Types:** Industry case studies and historical examples (The Witcher 3, Monopoly Go, Sony/Naughty Dog, Valve/HTC), third-party market research (Deloitte study), and financial performance data.

**Tables/Figures:** None present in this section.
